description: Migrate kernel logic from TypeScript to Rust sidecar. Split the service.rs monolith, convert to async, then migrate permissions, filesystem, process management, command resolution, tools, ACP sessions, and SQLite to Rust. TypeScript becomes a thin SDK (~2,500 lines down from ~10,400). See .agent/specs/typescript-to-rust-migration.md for the full design spec.
testPolicy: IMPORTANT: Do NOT run bare `pnpm test` — dev-shell and browser integration tests have pre-existing failures unrelated to this migration. Use scoped test commands specified in each story's acceptanceCriteria. The final story (US-029) addresses full pnpm test green.

userStories.title: Restore V8 isolate execution engine with kernel-backed polyfills
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the Node.js execution engine to run guest JS/TS inside V8 isolates with kernel-backed polyfills instead of spawning real host node processes. This is the single most critical architectural fix — without it, every guest process escapes the kernel to the real host.
acceptanceCriteria: Guest JS/TS code executes inside V8 isolates managed by the Rust sidecar, NOT in real host node child processes
All Node.js builtins (fs, net, child_process, dns, http, os, etc.) are kernel-backed polyfills that route through the kernel VFS, socket table, and process table
Module loading is fully intercepted — guest require()/import never falls through to real host modules
The sync RPC bridge (SharedArrayBuffer + Atomics.wait or equivalent) connects guest polyfills to the kernel
Guest code cannot observe it is running inside a V8 isolate — process.pid returns kernel PID, fs.readdirSync('/') shows kernel VFS, etc.
Command::new('node') is no longer used for guest code execution in javascript.rs
cargo check -p agent-os-execution passes
cargo test -p agent-os-execution -- --test-threads=1 passes
cargo check -p agent-os-sidecar passes
cargo test -p agent-os-sidecar -- --test-threads=1 passes
userStories.notes: CRITICAL FIX. V8 isolate execution engine restored. The agent-os-v8 binary (crates/v8-runtime/) manages V8 isolates. The execution engine (crates/execution/src/javascript.rs) now spawns V8 isolates instead of real node processes. Polyfills (18K lines) are bundled into crates/execution/assets/v8-bridge.js. IPC protocol (v8_ipc.rs), runtime manager (v8_host.rs), and bridge call translation (v8_runtime.rs) wire polyfill bridge calls to the sidecar's kernel handlers. Internal bridge calls (logging, module resolution, crypto, timers) are handled locally in the event bridge. The sidecar reads entrypoint files from the VFS and passes inline code to avoid module loading bridge calls during synchronous dispatch. Remaining integration: crash_isolation test hangs due to synchronous dispatch model not supporting concurrent bridge call handling — needs the async event loop (US-008's tokio::select!) to properly pump V8 bridge calls.

userStories.notes: Follow-up to US-008a. Three issues found in audit: (1) ~80 bridge functions (_netSocketConnectRaw, _netServerListenRaw, _dgramSocket*Raw, _networkHttp2*Raw, _crypto*, _tlsGetCiphersRaw, _ptySetRawMode, _batchResolveModules, etc.) exist in v8-bridge.js polyfills and in map_bridge_method/sidecar handlers, but are NOT registered in the V8 session's SYNC_BRIDGE_FNS/ASYNC_BRIDGE_FNS arrays (session.rs lines 743-802). userStories.notes: CRITICAL — setTimeout/setInterval are broken, which breaks all async guest code. The _scheduleTimer bridge function is async (in ASYNC_BRIDGE_FNS). userStories.title: Replace _networkFetchRaw with undici over kernel TCP sockets
userStories.description: As a developer, I want fetch() in V8 guest code to use undici (the real Node.js HTTP client) running over kernel-backed TCP sockets instead of a high-level _networkFetchRaw bridge call that bypasses the network stack.
acceptanceCriteria: Guest fetch() uses undici's fetch implementation, NOT a custom _networkFetchRaw bridge call
undici runs inside the V8 guest, making TCP connections via the kernel socket table (net.connect bridge calls)
HTTP request/response lifecycle goes through the kernel network stack: undici → net.connect → kernel socket table → host network adapter
DNS resolution for fetch() goes through the kernel DNS resolver (dns.lookup bridge), not host DNS
fetch() respects kernel network permissions — denied hosts/ports return network errors
Loopback connections (127.0.0.1) stay in-kernel like any other TCP connection
HTTPS/TLS fetch() uses the kernel TLS upgrade path (net.socket_upgrade_tls)
The old _networkFetchRaw bridge call is removed from SYNC_BRIDGE_FNS/ASYNC_BRIDGE_FNS
Guest code cannot bypass the kernel network stack via fetch()
fetch() works with streaming responses (ReadableStream body)
All existing fetch-related tests pass
cargo test -p agent-os-sidecar -- --test-threads=1 passes
userStories.notes: ARCHITECTURAL FIX. The current _networkFetchRaw is a high-level bridge call that delegates the entire HTTP request to the host, bypassing the kernel network stack. This means fetch() doesn't go through kernel permissions, kernel DNS, or kernel socket table. In contrast, net.connect, http.request, etc. properly use kernel TCP sockets. The fix is to run undici inside the guest V8 isolate, where it makes TCP connections via the kernel socket bridge (same as net.connect). This matches how real Node.js works — fetch() is implemented by undici which uses net.connect internally. undici is already in pnpm-lock.yaml (v7.24.6). Bundle it into v8-bridge.js and configure it to use the kernel net polyfill for TCP.
